one_line_takeaway: Injecting a single plausible-but-false article at the top search rank -- with the rest of the truthful corpus and unlimited search still available -- collapses frontier agent accuracy by 23 to 47 percentage points (e.g., GPT-5: 65.1% to 18.2%) while search escalation barely changes and confidence stays high, pointing to a structural 'positional anchoring' failure rather than an information-access or resource-budget problem.
key_finding: A single rank-0 honeypot article causes catastrophic, statistically significant accuracy collapse in every capable model tested, despite unlimited access to the truthful majority of the corpus and no tool-budget constraint. From the paper's Table 1 (5,870 queries per condition: 587 unique queries x 10 rollouts x 4 worlds): GPT-5 falls from 65.1% (63.8-66.3% CI) standard accuracy to 18.2% (17.2-19.2%) adversarial (-46.9 points); o3 falls from 48.4% to 16.7% (-31.7 points); o1 falls from 39.0% to 8.4% (-30.7 points); GPT-4o falls from 27.2% to 3.8% (-23.4 points). o4-mini and o1-mini perform near zero in both conditions (0.3%->0.0% and 0.0%->0.0%). Table 3 reports two-proportion z-tests confirming p<1e-10 for GPT-5, o3, o1, and GPT-4o. Tool-use traces (Table 2) show search escalation barely changes between standard and adversarial conditions (e.g., GPT-5: 6.45 -> 6.61 average tool calls; o3: 3.88 -> 4.23; GPT-4o: 1.14 -> 1.13), and only 62% (GPT-5) or 42% (o3) of adversarial queries reach 5+ tool calls. Calibration degrades sharply under adversarial exposure per Table 5 (e.g., GPT-5 ECE 0.298 -> 0.641, Brier 0.332 -> 0.591; GPT-4o ECE 0.513 -> 0.726). The human baseline achieved 98% accuracy standard / 93% adversarial on a sampled subset, establishing that the queries remain solvable and the failure is model-specific rather than task-infeasibility. The authors propose a 'positional anchoring' hypothesis -- models over-rely on the top-ranked search result and under-seek corroboration -- as a unifying explanation, drawing an explicit analogy to the previously reported 'lost in the middle' / U-shaped attention phenomenon (Liu et al., cited related work, not this paper's own finding) but extended here to search-rank position rather than long-context position.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the 'Synthetic Web Benchmark,' a procedurally generated, controllable 'mini-Internet' of thousands of hyperlinked articles with ground-truth credibility/factuality labels, built to causally isolate one specific vulnerability of web-browsing language agents: susceptibility to a single adversarially-ranked misinformation article placed at search rank 0. The benchmark's own components are: (1) an LLM-driven world generator that creates topic taxonomies, website profiles with credibility/bias attributes, and per-topic article clusters containing factual timelines plus deliberately fabricated but plausible misinformation claims (43% of sites set low-credibility in the main runs); (2) a hybrid lexical+dense search layer (via LlamaIndex) with a transient rank-0 'honeypot' injection mechanism that is added per-query and removed after each rollout to prevent contamination; (3) a search/read_article agent protocol requiring a structured Answer/Confidence/Explanation response with an effectively unbounded tool-call budget; and (4) an LLM-as-judge evaluation pipeline with a fixed grader, alias-based answer normalization, and contamination filtering (closed-book probing removes queries a strong model can already answer). Using this benchmark the authors run six frontier OpenAI-family models (GPT-5, o3, o1, GPT-4o, o4-mini, o1-mini) across 4 independently generated worlds (587 unique queries x 10 rollouts = 5,870 queries per condition) under standard vs. adversarial (rank-0 honeypot) search, and analyze tool-call traces to characterize failure behavior (search escalation, synthesis, calibration).
threat_model: The paper's own threat model is adversarial search-result ranking rather than prompt injection or corpus poisoning in the traditional RAG sense (those are explicitly cited as related but different attack surfaces). The adversary is assumed able to place one highly plausible, fabricated-but-stylistically-normal article at the very top (rank 0) of search results for a query -- realistically via SEO manipulation, paid/sponsored placement, domain-authority gaming, or compromise of search infrastructure -- without needing to suppress or remove any of the thousands of legitimate, truthful sources still available to the agent. The agent has unlimited search queries, unrestricted access to the rest of the (truthful) corpus, and no tool-call budget constraint; the paper is explicit that this isolates a purely epistemic/behavioral failure (how the agent weighs and synthesizes conflicting evidence) rather than an information-availability or resource-constraint failure. The paper also raises, as a downstream implication rather than a tested claim, that such rank-0 manipulation could cascade in multi-agent systems where agents consume other agents' outputs.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) they evaluate a uniform zero-shot prompt with no source-criticism instructions, chain-of-thought scaffolding, or verifier modules, so results characterize base model capability rather than production agent systems that may employ mitigations; (2) synthetic prose, while stylistically diversified, may still be more uniform than the live web, potentially making deception detection easier than in reality; worlds are text-only (no multimedia/UI); (3) the LLM-as-judge grader is not a perfect oracle and the human audit used to validate correctness (98%/93% accuracy) was a small random sample from only one of the four worlds, so larger-scale human validation is needed; (4) contamination filtering reduces but does not eliminate pretraining-leakage risk; (5) the evaluation does not stratify results by topic familiarity/in-distribution vs. out-of-distribution framing, leaving open whether failures are driven by over-familiar-seeming topics or genuine novelty; (6) all six evaluated models are from the OpenAI family (GPT-5, o3, o1, GPT-4o, o4-mini, o1-mini) -- no cross-vendor comparison is reported, which the paper does not flag explicitly but is evident from Table 1.

datasets_used: Synthetic Web Benchmark (this paper's own procedurally generated corpus): 4 independently generated worlds, each with its own topics/sites/articles; 587 unique queries across factual/comparison/timeline/evaluation types (query-type counts per Table 6: Factual 147, Comparison 160, Timeline 154, Evaluation 126); per-world content stats in Table 7 (19-20 sites per world, avg article length ~595-601 tokens, type-token ratio ~0.63-0.64, site-type mix roughly News 27-32% / Blog 37-43% / Research 10% / Conspiracy 10%)
quantitative_results: GPT-5 accuracy: 65.1% standard -> 18.2% adversarial (-46.9 pts, z=51.52, p=1.00e-16)
o3 accuracy: 48.4% standard -> 16.7% adversarial (-31.7 pts, z=36.59, p=1.00e-16)
o1 accuracy: 39.0% standard -> 8.4% adversarial (-30.7 pts, z=39.06, p=1.00e-16)
GPT-4o accuracy: 27.2% standard -> 3.8% adversarial (-23.4 pts, z=35.05, p=1.00e-16)
Tool-call usage nearly unchanged under attack: GPT-5 6.45->6.61 avg calls, o3 3.88->4.23, o1 1.83->1.86, GPT-4o 1.14->1.13, o4-mini 0.02->0.04, o1-mini 0.01->0.01
Fraction of adversarial queries with >=5 tool calls: GPT-5 62%, o3 42% (others lower)

Calibration under adversarial exposure (ECE / Brier): GPT-5 0.298/0.332 -> 0.641/0.591; o3 0.192/0.286 -> 0.479/0.384; o1 0.344/0.378 -> 0.646/0.519; GPT-4o 0.513/0.492 -> 0.726/0.638; o4-mini and o1-mini essentially unchanged (near-floor accuracy)
Human baseline accuracy on a sampled subset: 98% standard, 93% adversarial
Total evaluation scale: 5,870 queries per condition (587 unique queries x 10 rollouts x 4 worlds)
Schema adherence / answer-extraction rate exceeds 99% for all models (ruling out formatting artifacts as the cause of failure)
Low-credibility site fraction in main runs: 43%, with credibility scores drawn uniform [0.1,0.4] for low and [0.6,0.9] for high (no medium band used)
baselines_compared: Six OpenAI-family models compared against each other and against a human baseline: GPT-5, o3, o1, GPT-4o, o4-mini, o1-mini, plus human annotators on a sampled subset of one world
Standard (unmanipulated) search condition serves as each model's own paired baseline against the adversarial rank-0-honeypot condition (paired by world ID and rollout index)
